description: Deliberately egregious, multi-violation policy - a sanity-check fixture. If ComplyAgent fails to flag the majority of these as violations, something is fundamentally broken in the pipeline.

violations.explanation: Article 5(1)(c) (data minimisation) requires collection to be adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary. Collecting precise GPS location at all times, full contacts list, and photo metadata for a flash-sale shopping app is grossly excessive relative to the stated purpose.

Article 6(1) requires a valid legal basis for processing. 'However we see fit' and selling data without consent or any stated legal basis is a direct violation - there is no lawful basis articulated at all.
Article 5(1)(e) (storage limitation) prohibits indefinite retention with no purpose-based limit. 'Forever, including after deletion' is a direct, explicit violation.

Articles 15 (right of access) and 17 (right to erasure) cannot be waived by a user; GDPR rights are not contractually waivable. Stating users have no ability to access/correct/delete data, and that using the app waives GDPR rights, directly violates both articles.

Article 8(1) requires parental consent for children's data when relying on consent for information society services targeted at children. Explicitly collecting data from under-13 users with zero safeguards is a direct violation.

Articles 44 and 46 require an adequacy decision or appropriate safeguards for transfers outside the EEA. Transferring to countries with 'no data protection laws' and 'no additional safeguards' is a direct, explicit violation.

Article 32(1) requires appropriate technical measures, including encryption where appropriate, given the risk. Explicitly declining to encrypt stored personal data (including payment card details) for performance reasons is a direct violation.
Article 13(1)(a) requires identity and contact details of the controller to be provided. No contact method for privacy inquiries is given at all.
